claim: Personalized federated learning is captured by a two-level Bayesian hierarchical formulation in which inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets and thus the trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing.

The uncertainty-based formulation recovers standard federated averaging when inter-client variation is negligible and reduces to local-only learning when clients are weakly connected or a single client’s data quality dominates, thereby self-tuning the degree of personalization.

claim: The Fuzzy Jaccard Index is a scale-invariant similarity measure for ranked lists that replaces binary membership with fuzzy membership based on relative ranking scores and is bounded in [0,1] with value 1 if and only if the two rankings are identical.

title: Ensemble Kalman Methods for High-Dimensional Hierarchical Dynamic Space-Time Models
Ensemble Kalman Methods for High-Dimensional Hierarchical Dynamic Space-Time Models

claim: Embedding ensemble Kalman filtering and smoothing within Bayesian state-space inference enables approximate filtering and smoothing for high-dimensional, nonlinear, non-Gaussian hierarchical state-space models by using ensemble-based Gaussian updates to handle the high-dimensional state while inferring parameters.

In high-dimensional observation models with Gaussian noise, the variance of the logarithm of the particle (mixture) likelihood grows exponentially with state dimension at fixed ensemble size, implying severe weight degeneracy unless the ensemble size increases exponentially.

Approximating the prior with an ensemble Gaussian yields an ensemble Kalman likelihood that is biased but has substantially lower variance in high dimensions than particle-based likelihood estimates, improving the stability of parameter inference under finite computational budgets.

When states and parameters are forecast independent, the forecast distribution is parameter-free and can be computed once per time step, allowing reuse of a single forecast ensemble across parameter draws and greatly reducing computational cost.

claim: An adaptive Gaussian mixture filter that combines Kalman-type corrections with importance weighting via a bandwidth parameter and a tuning parameter α∈[0,1] interpolating between uniform and likelihood-driven weights provides a continuous bridge between ensemble Kalman and particle filtering while preserving non-Gaussian posterior structure and mitigating high-dimensional weight collapse.

Choosing α by minimizing a bias–variance tradeoff sets α equal to the ratio of the estimated effective ensemble size to the ensemble size and guarantees an effective ensemble size of at least 80% of the ensemble, thereby preventing degeneracy even with small kernel bandwidths.

In high-dimensional Lorenz-40 simulations with small ensembles, the adaptive Gaussian mixture approach yielded more accurate state estimates and closer approximations to non-Gaussian marginal posteriors than the standard ensemble Kalman filter.

The kernel bandwidth controls the balance between capturing non-Gaussian features and avoiding weight collapse, with small bandwidths yielding particle-filter-like skewed posteriors and large bandwidths yielding ensemble Kalman filter-like Gaussian posteriors.

claim: McPAT’s architectural power and area estimates for general-purpose out-of-order CPUs are dominated by abstraction and assumption errors—missing control-logic models, unrealistic porting and gating assumptions, and incorrect SMT resource duplication—that overestimate modeled structures while leaving large fractions of unit power unrepresented.

Adjusting McPAT’s modeling assumptions to reflect shared SMT structures and realistic register-file and instruction-buffer port configurations improves estimation fidelity more than refining published configuration parameters alone.
Validating only total core or chip power can mask large internal errors due to error canceling, whereas unit-level or finer-grained validation reveals them.

Leakage power accuracy is the primary determinant of steady-state metrics such as average chip temperature and static IR drop, whereas dynamic power accuracy is critical for spatial hotspot identification and temporal inductive noise prediction.
Incorporating models for a limited set of high-impact unmodeled macros would allow McPAT to account for the majority of unit power within its framework while retaining architectural-level generality.

title: Evolution of the social network of scientific collaborations
Evolution of the social network of scientific collaborations

claim: Scientific co-authorship networks are scale-free and exhibit a time-dependent crossover in their degree distribution from a steeper early-time tail to a shallower long-time tail.

Network evolution is governed by preferential attachment at two levels, with new authors favoring highly connected collaborators and new collaborations between existing authors occurring with probability approximately proportional to the product of their degrees.
Internal link formation drives a growth in the average number of collaborators per author over time, contradicting models that assume a constant average degree.

Apparent decreases in average path length and increases in the largest connected component observed in cumulative records can arise from incomplete temporal sampling rather than genuine contraction or percolation of the full network.

A growth model combining external and internal preferential attachment captures the observed time evolution of collaboration networks and underscores the central role of internal links in shaping topology and scaling.

claim: A generative adversarial framework that samples latent variables from a normalized statistical shape model and passes only geometrically warped outputs to a discriminator enforces canonical-frame geometry and creates an implicit connection between the shape prior and the generated image.

A local appearance preservation loss, constructed by mirroring and perturbing shapes and comparing their warped outputs in a canonical frame, promotes shape-consistent generations that maintain appearance across geometry-preserving transformations.

The geometry-aware formulation can be integrated into arbitrary GAN architectures, leverages domain-specific priors including symmetry and local invariance, and remains effective with relatively small annotated datasets.
Conditioning on fiducial-point-based shapes enables application to diverse object categories with landmark annotations beyond human faces.

Efficient detection of contagious outbreaks in massive metropolitan encounter networks
Efficient detection of contagious outbreaks in massive metropolitan encounter networks

claim: In a city-scale temporal encounter network derived from transit co-presence, friend sensors chosen as neighbors of randomly selected individuals detect simulated contagious outbreaks earlier than the population despite relying only on local network information.

Selecting sensors globally by highest encounter degree yields earlier and more reliable detection lead-times than friend sensors and performs at least as well as selections based on travel frequency, encounter entropy, or k-shell centrality in this metropolitan contact setting.

The friendship paradox extends to multiple behavioral and structural metrics in temporal encounter networks, with neighbors of individuals exhibiting higher degree, travel frequency, k-shell index, and encounter entropy than randomly selected individuals.

Sensor set size modulates performance, with smaller high-degree sensor groups providing longer but more variable lead-times, whereas the average lead-time of friend sensors is essentially independent of sample size.

Monitoring a very small fraction of the population selected by highest encounter degree can deliver substantial and reliable early warning of contagious spread in metropolitan transit encounter networks.

claim: An iterative generative optimization that alternates training a long short‑term memory recurrent neural network on top‑performing sequences and sampling candidates from the learned distribution can autonomously identify dynamical decoupling sequences that exceed standard DD families for a one‑qubit memory under finite‑width, piecewise‑constant, 100% duty‑cycle pulses while treating the performance function as a black box.
Long short‑term memory models capture the multi‑scale structure of effective decoupling sequences and outperform simple n‑gram models in generating high‑quality candidates.
Using Pauli gates as the control alphabet yields better optimization outcomes than using randomly chosen unitaries because first‑order decoupling can be achieved with much shorter sequences.

Segmentation of Bone in Clinical Knee MRI Using Texture-Based Geodesic Active Contours

claim: Vector-valued geodesic active contours that combine intensity and local variance gradients with an image-dependent balloon force enable robust segmentation of trabecular femur and tibia regions in clinical knee MRI from interior initializations, mitigating leakage and instability typical of intensity-only snakes under noise and intensity inhomogeneity.

A constant outward correction applied to the converged contour compensates for inward bias caused by windowed variance computation and gradient smoothing, improving alignment with the anatomical boundary.
The approach is tailored to trabecular bone near joints rather than cortical bone and operates independently on 2D slices whose results can be stacked for volumetric depiction.
